text.title: Named Flows are the Sole Trigger Surface
text.summary: GitLab Duo's named flows (Developer, Code Review, Software Development, CI/CD) are the only allowed agent-trigger surface; raw API drivers or external webhooks that mutate MRs are forbidden.

section.title: Label-triggered flows, project-permission inheriting
section.p: Routing every agent run through a named flow keeps the trigger visible in the project's automation surface and inherits protected-branch and approval-rule enforcement automatically.

rule.statement: Every coding agent action against a GitLab project MUST be invoked through a named Duo flow declared in `.gitlab/duo-flows.yaml`. Triggers MUST be either (a) issue label transitions (`agent:implement`, `agent:review`) or (b) scheduled pipelines. Direct API calls to `merge_requests` from outside Duo are forbidden in production projects. The flow definition MUST set `require_human_review: true` for any change touching a protected branch and MUST NOT exceed the assignee's project role; protected-branch settings, CODEOWNERS, and approval rules apply to flow output exactly as they apply to human MRs.
rule.rationale: GitLab Duo's flow contract makes the platform — not external webhooks — the system of record for "an agent did this". Side webhooks or external runners bypass the audit log and protected-branch enforcement, recreating the shared-token anti-pattern Duo was built to retire. Tying triggers to label transitions also gives auditors one surface to review: the project's label history is the agent invocation log.

example.code: # .gitlab/duo-flows.yaml flows: developer: trigger: issue_label_added label: "agent:implement" require_human_review: true output: merge_request auto_link_issue: true # MR body gets "Closes #issue" respect: [protected_branches, codeowners, approval_rules] code_review: trigger: merge_request_opened standards: [security, perf, style]
example.note: Two named flows, label-triggered, project rules inherited; no external webhook in sight.

example.code: # external GitHub Actions runner reaching into GitLab on: schedule steps: - run: | curl -X POST $GITLAB_URL/api/v4/merge_requests \ -H "PRIVATE-TOKEN: $GITLAB_PAT" \ --data "..." # bypasses Duo entirely
example.note: External runner, admin PAT, MR appears with no link to a flow; protected-branch checks happen, but the audit log says "PAT user" with zero context.

antipattern.description: Agent flow defined in Duo but invoked via a Slack slash command that takes the user's input verbatim and passes it to the runner.
antipattern.reason: Slash command bypasses label-trigger discoverability; auditors looking at the project's label history cannot find the run, so reconstructing what happened requires correlating Slack logs with GitLab logs.
